coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots regulate the interface electron transfer and catalytic kinetics of Pt-based alloys catalyst for highly efficient hydrogen oxidation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jie
Zhou, Yunjie
Nie, Haodong
Wei, Kaiqiang
Huang, Hui
Liao, Fan
Liu, Yang
Shao, Mingwang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: The regulation of interface electron-transfer and catalytic kinetics is very important to design the efficient electrocatalyst for alkaline hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR). Here, we show the Pt-Ni alloy nanoparticles (PtNi2) have an enhanced HOR activity compared with single component Pt catalyst. While, the interface electron-transfer kinetics of PtNi2 catalyst exhibits a very wide electron-transfer speed distribution. When combined with carbon dots (CDs), the interface charge transfer of PtNi2-CDs composite is optimized, and then the PtNi2-5 mg CDs exhibits about 2.67 times and 4.04 times higher mass and specific activity in 0.1 M KOH than that of 20% commercial Pt/C. In this system, CDs also contribute to trapping H+ and H2O generated during HOR, tuning hydrogen binding energy (HBE), and regulating interface electron transfer. This work provides a deep understanding of the interface catalytic kinetics of Pt-based alloys towards highly efficient HOR catalysts design.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pt-based alloys
Carbon dots
Interface electron transfer
Interface catalytic kinetics
Hydrogen oxidation reaction

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), the National MCF Energy R&D Program of China (2018YFE0306105), the Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), the Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, and the 111 Project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM (inset: HRTEM image and particle size distribution of CDs) and HRTEM images of CDs; (c and d) TEM and HRTEM images of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C; (e–h) STEM-EDX elemental mappings of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C.

(a) TPV curve of PtNi2/C; (b) average electron relaxation time of metal-based samples; (c) TPV curve of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C; (d) average electron relaxation time (τ ave) of PtNi2/C with different amounts of CDs.
TPV curves, FFT curves and CWT patterns of PtNi2/C (a and b), CDs (c and d) and PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C (e and f).

HOR performance of catalysts. (a) HOR polarization curves with iR-compensation obtained on PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, PtNi2/C, and commercial Pt/C; (b) HOR polarization curves of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C with different rotation speeds ranging from 400 to 2500 r min−1; (c) corresponding Koutecky-Levich plots at an overpotential of 50 mV; (d) HOR/HER micro-polarization region of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, PtNi2/C, and commercial Pt/C; (e) HOR/HER Tafel plots of the kinetic current density of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, PtNi2/C, and commercial Pt/C catalysts in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH; (f) j 0 and j 0 normalized to Pt loading of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, PtNi2 and commercial Pt/C catalysts; (g) mass activity and specific activity of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, PtNi2/C, and commercial Pt/C catalysts; (h) anti-CO poisoning performance of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C; (i) anti-CO poisoning performance of commercial Pt/C.

(a) H+ adsorption capacity (mmol g−1) of CDs; (b) the H upd electrochemical desorption curves of PtNi2/C and PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C; (c) CV of CDs in Ar-saturated anhydrous acetonitrile; (d) different concentrations of CDs solution.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots regulate the interface electron transfer and catalytic kinetics of Pt-based alloys catalyst for highly efficient hydrogen oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macau SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering
Macau University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macau SAR, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CDs regulate the interface electron transfer and catalytic kinetics of PtNi2-CDs effectively, and then PtNi2-5 mg CDs exhibit about 2.67 times and 4.04 times higher mass and specific HOR activity than that of commercial Pt/C.

The regulation of interface electron-transfer and catalytic kinetics is very important to design the efficient electrocatalyst for alkaline hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR). Here, we show the Pt-Ni alloy nanoparticles (PtNi2) have an enhanced HOR activity compared with single component Pt catalyst. While, the interface electron-transfer kinetics of PtNi2 catalyst exhibits a very wide electron-transfer speed distribution. When combined with carbon dots (CDs), the interface charge transfer of PtNi2-CDs composite is optimized, and then the PtNi2-5 mg CDs exhibits about 2.67 times and 4.04 times higher mass and specific activity in 0.1 M KOH than that of 20% commercial Pt/C. In this system, CDs also contribute to trapping H+ and H2O generated during HOR, tuning hydrogen binding energy (HBE), and regulating interface electron transfer. This work provides a deep understanding of the interface catalytic kinetics of Pt-based alloys towards highly efficient HOR catalysts design.

ce:section.ce:para: Anion-exchange membrane fuel cells (AEMFCs) are considered to be one of the effective ways for hydrogen energy conversion, which is based on the efficient hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [1–3]. In general, the Pt-based electrocatalysts are regarded as the most efficient electrocatalyst for HOR, and the design strategy for the popular catalyst is to reduce the loading amount of Pt in the catalysts [4–6]. Recently, much efforts have been paid and many low Pt-loaded catalyst have been fabricated by different strategies, such as synthesizing ultra-small platinum nanoparticles, preparing nanocrystals with high-index facets and alloying platinum with other metals [7–10]. When Pt alloyed with other metals, the electronic structure of Pt will be adjusted and then the alloy catalyst will exhibit high activity, which might be attributed to the optimized adsorption/desorption of species and structural lattice strain effects [11–13]. While, in this field, Pt catalysts often suffer from sluggish kinetic in alkaline media, and the HOR catalytic performance may in general be reduced in an order of two when changing the electrolyte from acid to base [14,15]. Also, the regulation of electrons transfer and interface catalytic kinetics is extremely important to design the efficient electrocatalyst for alkaline HOR [16,17]. An ideal HOR electrocatalyst should have the qualities of good electron transfer, rapid H+ desorption and fast H2O desorption from active sites (Scheme S1). To understand the interface electron transfer process, fine tuning the intrinsic properties of catalysts by external stimuli signals is an important method. Stimuli-response technique receives wide popularity because it helps to adjust the physical and chemical properties of the catalyst, thereby improving its catalytic performance [18,19].

Carbon dots (CDs) with many unique physical and chemical properties are often used as co-electrocatalysts and enhance the overall catalytic performance [20–22]. CDs can act as electron acceptor or electron donor and optimize the reaction intermediates adsorption, then eventually promote the catalytic process [23–25]. Herein, we show the PtNi2 having an enhanced HOR activity compared with the single component Pt. While, the interface electron-transfer kinetics of PtNi2 catalyst exhibits a very wide electron-transfer speed distribution. When combined with CDs, the interface charge transfer of PtNi2-CDs composite is optimized, and then the PtNi2-5 mg CDs exhibits about 2.67 times and 4.04 times higher mass and specific activity in 0.1 M KOH than that of 20% commercial Pt/C. Due to the multifunctionality of CDs, including capture of H+ and H2O generated during HOR process, tuning hydrogen binding energy (HBE), and a bridge to regulate interface electron transfer process. This work provides a deep understanding of the interface catalytic kinetics of Pt-based alloys electrocatalysts for alkaline HOR.

A series of carbon (XC-72R) supported octahedral PtNi x nanoparticles with (without) CDs are synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method (Experimental details are shown in the Supporting Information; ICP-AES results of series catalysts are listed in Table S1). Now we analyze the structure of typical catalysts of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C in details. Firstly, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CDs and PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C are showed in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1(a) exhibits TEM image of CDs, and the size of CDs is about 3 nm and uniformly dispersed. And Fig. 1(b) shows crystal lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is in good agreement with the (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon. The well-dispersed octahedral PtNi2 nanoparticles are successfully prepared through direct one-step solvothermal method, as shown in Fig. 1(c). HRTEM image in Fig. 1(d) reveals a well-defined lattice spacing of 0.22 and 0.21 nm, corresponding with the (111) plane of PtNi2 and (100) plane of CDs, respectively, which are coincident with the literatures [26,27]. This indicates that CDs are distributed around the PtNi2. TEM and HRTEM images of PtNi2 without CDs are also presented in Fig. S1. And the TEM images of other samples are shown in Fig. S2. It could be seen that all the Pt-based samples are octahedral nanoparticles with similar particle sizes (less than 10 nm), however, the shape of the particles in the Ni/C is irregular and much larger than those of Pt-based samples. The scanning transmission electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM-EDX) elemental mapping images in Fig. 1(e–h) confirm the presence of Pt and Ni elements, which are distributed uniformly in octahedral nanoparticles.

The crystalline phase and structure of these Pt-based catalysts are further revealed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. S3 shows the XRD patterns of CDs, PtNi2/C and PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, and the XRD patterns of catalysts with different metal radios of PtNi x /C and PtNi2/C with different amounts of CDs are shown in Fig. S4. Pt/C-syn displays the major peaks at 39.89°, 46.40°, and 67.71°, which are typical Pt with the phase of face centered cubic and consistent with JCPDS card No. 04-0802 [28,29]. Moreover, with the increase of Ni, the diffraction peaks shift towards high angle, indicating the Pt atoms substituted with Ni [30]. Fig. S4(b) plots the XRD patterns of PtNi2 with different amounts of CDs, where all of these patterns are similar with PtNi2 due to the small amounts of carbon dots.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is employed to acquire the elemental and electronic states information of the catalysts. Taking PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C as an example, the high-resolution of Pt 4f are shown in Fig. S5(a), the binding energies at 71.5 and 75.0 eV are related to metallic Pt0 4f 7/2 and Pt0 4f 5/2, respectively. And the two weak peaks correspond to Pt2+ 4f 7/2 and Pt2+ 4f 5/2, which are located at 72.6 and 76.9 eV, respectively [31,32]. The high-resolution Ni 2p spectra were examined to confirm the Ni surface condition (Fig. S5b). For PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, two peaks at 853 and 870.3 eV could be distributed to Ni0 2p 3/2 and Ni0 2p 5/2. Binding energies at 856.35 and 874.2 eV could be attributed to Ni2+, which are accompanied by two shakeup satellite signals at 861.7 and 880.3 eV [33,34]. Compared with Ni/C and Pt/C-syn, the binding energy shifts to higher and lower energies, respectively, which could be due to the formation of alloy and difference in electronegativity (Ni: 1.91, Pt: 2.28) [35]. Difference in electronegativity would lead to the transfer of electron from Ni to Pt, which could further result in the downward motion of the d-band center position and enhance the catalyst activity [29,36]. In order to confirm that the inside of the sample is in a metallic state, argon ion beam etching is applied to remove the surface atoms, as shown in Fig. S6. Although the change in Pt signals is not obvious, the signals of metallic Ni gradually become the main signal with the disappearance of Ni2+ signals, confirming that the inside of the catalyst is a metallic state. The full survey spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. S7(a), which reveals that the C and O elements in the CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s display three peaks at 284.6, 286.7 and 288.7 eV, which could be assigned to C–C, C–O and C = O bonds (Fig. S7b). In Fig. S7(c), two peaks at 531.8 and 532.6 eV present C–O and C = O bonds, respectively. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S8, the peaks of CDs are located at 3420, 1720, 1620 and 1245 cm−1, which are the vibrations of O–H, C = O, C = C and C–O–C bonds [37,38].

Next, a stimuli-response method, namely, the transient photo-induced voltage (TPV) tests are carried out to reveal the electron transfer behavior within electrocatalyst interface (the experimental details are shown in the Supporting Information). Here, 355 nm laser is employed as an external stimuli signal, which may excite plasmon-induced hot electrons on the surface of metals. According to Fig. S9, plasmon resonance of Pt and Ni would occur at 355 nm and hot electrons could be transferred out to generate stimuli-response current. The typical TPV decay curve of PtNi2/C is shown in Fig. 2 (a), the signal of PtNi2/C decreases rapidly in the early stage, and a very slow process after the initial sharp decay (<0.02 ms), and the complete signal decay time is up to 11.0 ms (inset figure of Fig. 2b). The average electron relaxation time (τ ave, 0.0257 ms) is calculated to determine the overall relaxation time constant. The comparison of the complete TPV signals of all the metal-based samples (Pt/C-syn, Ni/C, and PtNi x /C) is shown in Fig. S10 and Fig. 2(b). The TPV decay curve of Pt/C-syn shows relatively even decrease and shortest decay time of 6 ms, in contrast, the Ni/C possess the longer decay process than PtNi2/C (11 ms), and nearly longest time is needed (16 ms for Ni/C sample). As shown in Fig. 2(b), Pt/C-syn shows the biggest τ ave (0.3644 ms), in contrast, the Ni/C possesses nearly the smallest τ ave (0.0220 ms). Clearly, with the increase of Ni, the time needed to the complete decay is growing and the τ ave of the samples is decreasing. The huge difference between fast electron transfer process and slow electron transfer process is emerging with the increase of Ni, which indicates the fast electron transfer occurs on Ni sites and slow electron transfer occurs on Pt sites.

In PtNi alloy, as the HOR active sites, Pt sites, suffer from slow electron transfer, which is unfriendly to the electrocatalytic process. Notably, the TPV decay curves of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C exhibit entirely different decay curve compared with PtNi2/C (Fig. 2c), the decay process of the curve is relatively uniform. A much shorter decay time (6.5 ms) is needed and the τ ave is about 0.3792 ms. The signal of TPV decay time for PtNi2/C with 3 mg/7 mg CDs loading (decay curves shown in Fig. S11) is also greatly shortened, only 7.5 ms for PtNi2-3 mg CDs/C and 6 ms for PtNi2-7 mg CDs/C. The TPV signal τ ave of PtNi2/C with 3 or 7 mg CDs loading and CDs are also extended, 0.3100 ms for PtNi2-3 mg CDs/C and 0.3788 ms for PtNi2-7 mg CDs/C, as shown in Fig. 2(d). Detail data about decay time and τ ave are listed in Table S2. It is apparent that the decay processes of PtNi2-CDs/C are uniform, which should be attributed to the effective regulation of the interface electron-transfer by CDs.

We further analyze the interface charge transfer processes of different samples based on their TPV decay curves. As known, the fast Fourier transform (FFT) is a tool that breaks a waveform (a function or signal) into an alternate representation, characterized by sine and cosines. When we plot the magnitude of signal as a function of frequency, the spikes in magnitude correspond to the signal frequency components of different frequency (Hz). Fourier transform can only identify the frequency components in the signal, but cannot identify when these frequency components occur. Continuous wavelet transform (CWT) is a frequency-time transform used to analyze non-static signals. In our system, the TPV relaxation curve is a typical non-static signal. Thus, the continuous wavelet analysis provides a time-scale/frequency-scale analysis of these TPV relaxation curves, in which, base function of bior3.9 (the graphs of bior3.9 shown in Fig. S12) is used to analyse the time–frequency characteristics of these TPV decay curves [39]. The magnitudes of the components in the colorbar are essentially their amplitudes even though they are at different scales.

In Fig. 3 , we show the FFT curves and CWT patterns of PtNi2/C, CDs and PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, respectively. The results of FFT on the three TPV decay curves are shown in Fig. 3(a–e). These FFT curves show the maximum amplitude when the frequency is approximate to zero. Smooth curves without any obvious peak indicate that there is no obvious static and periodic frequency component present in the TPV relaxation signals, both the curve feature of TPV signal and Fourier transform results suggest that the TPV curve has obvious non static characteristics. CWT spectra of PtNi2/C, Ni/C and PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C are shown in Fig. 3(b–f), and their corresponding 3D CWT patterns are shown in Fig. S13. It is clear that CDs have a strong effect on the interface charge transfer of the PtNi2/C. Also, the FFT and CWT analyses on PtNi2-3 mg CDs/C and PtNi2-7 mg CDs/C TPV decay curves are carried out and shown in Figs. S14 and S15, respectively. Notably, after combining with CDs, the signal frequency in range of 0–4500 Hz changes to 0–150 Hz, indicating the wide interface electron-transfer speed distribution of PtNi2/C catalysts is sharply narrowed by CDs.

Fig. S16–S21 display the FFT and CWT patterns of Pt/C-syn, Pt3Ni/C, Pt2Ni/C, PtNi/C, PtNi3/C and Ni/C. All these FFT curves show the maximum amplitude when the frequency is approximate to zero, suggesting that these TPV curves have obvious non static characteristics. While, for CWT patterns, the TPV decay signal frequency is changed remarkably with the increasing amount of Ni, including 0–150 Hz for Pt/C-syn, 0–200 Hz for Pt3Ni/C, 0–250 Hz for Pt2Ni/C, 0–3500 Hz for PtNi/C, and 0–4500 Hz for PtNi2/C, PtNi3/C and Ni/C. When combined with CDs, a much narrower electron-transfer speed distribution in PtNi2-CDs/C catalyst is obtained, which is helpful to promote their electrocatalytic performance.

Next, we evaluate the activity of these catalyst for HOR in alkaline media. The catalysts are placed in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution to evaluate the HOR electrocatalytic activity. Fig. S22 shows a comparison of cyclic voltammograms (CVs) in H2 vs. Ar saturated electrolyte, confirming the activity of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C towards the HOR. The polarization curves of the other PtNi x /C and PtNi2/C are shown in Fig. S23(a). The performance of Pt and Ni after forming an alloy is better than Pt/C-syn, which may benefit from the optimization of electronic charge density and adsorption energy [33,40,41]. The performance is optimal when the Pt to Ni radio is 1:2, and a dropped HOR performance is obtained as the Ni increases and Pt decreases. The influences of CDs in enhancing PtNi2/C catalytic activity are also investigated to determine the best amounts of CDs, as shown in Fig. S23(b), and the optimal amount of CDs is 5 mg.

Polarization curves for the HOR of commercial Pt/C, PtNi2/C and PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C are given in Fig. 4 (a), which reveal that PtNi2 and PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C catalysts both possess higher current densities than Pt/C and higher HOR catalytic activity. The HOR activity of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C is also higher than that of PtNi2, demonstrating the addition of CDs has an improvement of HOR performance. The HOR polarization curves on PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C catalyst with different rotating rates are shown in Fig. 4(b). Due to the promoted mass transport, the current densities increase with the higher rotating rates. The square root of reciprocal rotation rates and the corresponding reciprocal current densities of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C are also plotted in Fig. 4(c) with obvious linear relationship. The fitting slope is 4.70 cm2 mA−1 s−1/2, which agrees with the theoretic value and proves the two-electron process [42]. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the exchange currents density (j 0) of the catalysts is calculated from the linear fitting of the micro-polarization region (Fig. 4f left), PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C gets the highest j 0 (3.33 mA cm−2) compared with PtNi2/C (2.79 mA cm−2) and commercial Pt/C (1.40 mA cm−2). Whereafter, the kinetic current densities of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, PtNi2/C and commercial Pt/C catalysts as a logarithmic function versus the potential in Tafel regions are plotted in Fig. 4(e). The exchange currents density is normalized to Pt loading to obtain accurate comparisons, Fig. 4(f) (right) shows that PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C with a j 0 of 0.9 A mgPt −1, which is 1.234 and 3.214 folds than PtNi2/C (0.729 A mgPt −1) and the commercial Pt/C (0.28 A mgPt −1), respectively. At 50 mV, PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C catalyst displays a geometric kinetic current density (jk ) of 10.16 mA cm−2, where the PtNi2/C and commercial Pt/C catalyst show 8.97 and 5.34 mA cm−2, respectively. For more accurate assessment of HOR activity, the ECSA of different samples is determined through H upd (Fig. S24) [36,43]. The calculated ECSA values are displayed in Table S3. The mass activity (MA) and specific activity (SA) are normalized to the ECSA values to reveal intrinsic HOR activities, which are plotted in Fig. 4(g). The PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C catalyst is 2.67 folds mass activity and 4.04 folds specific activity than commercial Pt/C. Moreover, compared with other reported HOR electrocatalysts, the PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C is highly active H2 oxidation catalyst with prospects in alkaline media (Table S4).

Generally, the vast majority of H2 is produced by reforming natural gas, which will inevitably introduce trace amounts of CO impurity in H2. Even if 10 ppm of CO could rapidly occupy the active sites of nano-catalysts and reduce the efficiency of hydrogen fuel cells significantly, especially in the Pt-based catalysts [44]. Hence, it is necessary to evaluate the CO-tolerant performance of HOR catalysts. Fig. 4(h) shows the LSV curves of commercial PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C under H2 and H2 + 1% CO, it could be found that the performance degradation is very small in the presence of 1% CO. In contrast, the performance of commercial Pt/C decreases near 50% when applied 1% CO into H2 (Fig. 4i). The CO-tolerance of PtNi2/C is plotted in Fig. S25, which reveals that the PtNi2/C possesses a poor tolerance than that of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C, but better than Pt/C. And the CO-tripping in Fig. S26 also confirm this point. It could be seen that PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C displays the lowest peak voltage (0.7242 V), which is earlier than those of PtNi2/C (0.7362 V) and Pt/C (0.7442 V). The excellent tolerance to CO toxicity of the catalyst might be attributed to two major reasons: (1) Reducing CO adsorption energy by alloying the Ni with Pt, and (2) carbon dots could absorb CO molecular and prevent the CO from absorbing on the surface of the catalyst [5,45,46].

In the following study, we further investigate other functions of CDs in present PtNi2/C catalyst. Plenty of H+ would be produced on the catalytic sites during the HOR process, hence, fast transfer of the H+ from catalytic sites is particularly significant. CDs could trap H+ and the H+ adsorption capacity (mmol g−1) of CDs is shown in Fig. 5 (a) [24]. It could be clearly seen that CDs exhibit excellent H+ adsorption capacity, which could reach 40 mmol g−1 in the early 10 min and a total of 70 mmol g−1 after 60 min. When combined with CDs, parts of H+ generated at reaction sites would be captured onto CDs due to the adsorption ability of CDs, and then react with hydroxyl ions, making the active sites unoccupied and HOR performance enhanced. As shown in Fig. 5(b), compared with PtNi2/C, PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C shows a lower potential of H upd peak, proving the weaker HBE of PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C and HBE tuning effects of CDs. The strong HBE is major contributors to sluggish HOR kinetics in alkaline electrolyte, and weaker HBE is more conducive to HOR [47]. Thus, CDs optimize the catalytic kinetics from the perspective of HBE. Due to the H2 lose electrons during HOR process, the PtNi2 nanoparticles would be electron-rich, which is unfavorable for H2 further oxidation. CDs, as a charge storage container, could trap electrons and release electrons [23,48]. CDs would capture electrons from PtNi2 alloys and release to carbon support (XC-72R), eventually the HOR performance would be enhanced. There are several redox peaks in the CV curve of CDs in Ar-saturated anhydrous acetonitrile, confirming that the electronic storage and release capabilities of CDs (Fig. 5c). Besides, CDs with abundant oxygen containing functional groups usually exhibit good hydrophilicity [49]. The optical image in Fig. 5(d) presents different concentrations of CDs solutions, proving the hydrophilic of our synthesized CDs. In spite of transfer of protons, there were also some H2O generated on the alloys, CDs would absorb the H2O from alloys due to extremely hydrophilic of CDs. The good hydrophilicity of CDs is also helpful to improve the HOR activity of the catalyst.

Based on all above results, we propose a schematic diagram of this catalyst during HOR process, shown in Scheme S2. There were several functions of CDs that promote HOR performance, which could be summarized as the following: (I) CDs regulate interface electron transfer process, promoting the electrocatalytic activity; (II) CDs adsorb the produced protons on the catalytic sites during HOR process; (III) CDs have the HBE tuning effect; (IV) CDs, as a charge storage container, could trap electrons from PtNi2 alloy and release electrons to XC-72R; (V) CDs absorb the H2O from alloys due to their hydrophilic nature. Overall, CDs regulated the interface charge kinetics in several ways, enhancing HOR activity effectively.

In summary, a series of PtNi x alloys and PtNi2 with different amounts CDs are synthesized and their catalytic activities for HOR are also evaluated in details. When the Ni content of alloys increasing, the interface electron-transfer kinetics of PtNi x catalysts exhibit wider electron-transfer speed distribution. When combined with CDs, this kind of wide interface electron-transfer speed distribution is sharply narrowed by CDs. CDs also show several other functions on the regulation the interface catalytic kinetics, including adsorption of H+, tuning HBE, trapping electrons/H2O from alloys and regulating interface electron transfer process. In terms of mass and specific activity for HOR, PtNi2/C outperforms commercial Pt/C about 2.32-folds and 3.38-folds. Furthermore, the PtNi2-5 mg CDs/C shows about 2.67 times and 4.04 times higher mass and specific activity for HOR than those commercial Pt/C in 0.1 M KOH. This work provides a new design concept to obtain high-performance electrocatalysts from the regulation of electron transmission process and interface catalytic kinetics perspective.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), the National MCF Energy R&D Program of China (2018YFE0306105), the Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), the Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, and the 111 Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2021.07.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
